primary_diagnosis.name: Vasovagal (reflex) syncope - classic emotional/blood-phobia triggered faint, uncomplicated (no injury from the fall)

pathophysiology_summary: Vasovagal (neurally mediated reflex) syncope is a transient, self-limiting loss of consciousness caused by a reflex fall in blood pressure and/or heart rate, producing brief global cerebral hypoperfusion. This is a textbook emotionally/visually triggered variant: the sight of blood/a surgical procedure on television, in a susceptible squeamish young adult standing in a warm room, activates a strong afferent reflex that produces vasodilatation and paradoxical bradycardia (a Bezold-Jarisch-type response), dropping cerebral perfusion. Every hallmark benign feature is present: a rich, distinct autonomic prodrome (nausea, profuse diaphoresis, a sensation of bodily warmth, visual 'tunnelling', tinnitus/roaring in the ears), an identifiable emotional/situational trigger, bystander-observed marked pallor with loss of postural tone (a limp fall, not stiffening), and rapid, complete, spontaneous recovery within about 20 seconds of adopting a supine posture with no prolonged postictal confusion. Post-event fatigue and a 'washed-out' feeling are also typical of reflex syncope. The clinical task is to discriminate this benign reflex faint from dangerous causes while remaining safe: cardiac/arrhythmic or structural syncope is characteristically sudden and unheralded ('drop attack'), exertional, or associated with palpitations/chest pain and a family history of sudden death - none of which are present; seizure is argued against by the classic autonomic prodrome, profound pallor, rapid recovery without postictal confusion, and the absence of tongue-biting, incontinence and tonic-clonic movements; orthostatic hypotension is argued against because the faint followed an emotional visual trigger rather than the immediate act of rising, and (on clinician measurement, sealed) active-stand orthostatic vitals showed no significant postural drop. The fall was onto carpet with no injury detected on examination, so - unlike a syncope complicated by a head strike - there is no additional mechanical hazard here. A resting 12-lead ECG is a reasonable low-yield baseline screen to exclude an occult arrhythmic substrate (e.g. long QT, pre-excitation, Brugada pattern) in any first syncope, but formal investigation such as tilt-table testing is not indicated for a classic uncomplicated first episode. Over telehealth the reflex pattern can be confidently recognised and safety-netted, but the neurological examination, cardiac auscultation, active-stand orthostatic vitals and the baseline ECG require in-person review.

differential.evidence_basis: A transient loss of consciousness triggered by the sight of blood on TV, with a nausea/tunnel-vision prodrome and rapid recovery on lying flat, is the classic emotionally triggered reflex-faint pattern from the opening account.

Any syncope mandates active exclusion of a cardiac cause - a first faint could be the only warning of an arrhythmia or structural heart disease. Screen for exertional/no-warning onset, palpitations, chest pain and family history of sudden death.
Transient loss of consciousness raises seizure, but must be distinguished by prodrome, colour change, recovery time and postictal state, tongue-biting, incontinence and any tonic-clonic movements.
Postural mechanisms are considered, but an emotional visual trigger (rather than symptoms immediately on rising) points to a reflex faint; requires active-stand vitals to distinguish.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The AI Doctor should treat vasovagal syncope as the leading diagnosis while still screening for dangerous cardiac/exertional syncope and seizure, and should confirm there was no injury from the fall. Accepting 'just a faint' without screening the cardiac red flags is a reasoning failure at this stage.

differential.evidence_basis: Autonomic prodrome (DI-001), an emotional blood/surgery visual trigger in a warm room (DI-002), rapid complete recovery within ~20 seconds without confusion (DI-003), bystander pallor with a limp fall (DI-004), and a negative dangerous-syncope screen - no exertional/no-warning onset, no palpitations/chest pain (DI-005), no family history of sudden death (DI-007), no tongue-biting/incontinence/jerking (DI-006) - together indicate a benign reflex faint. The fall was onto carpet with no injury detected (DI-008), so there is no additional mechanical complication.

The history argues strongly against a cardiac cause, but a baseline resting 12-lead ECG remains appropriate for any first syncope to exclude an occult arrhythmic substrate; it cannot be obtained via telehealth.
A classic autonomic prodrome, marked pallor, rapid recovery without postictal confusion, a limp (non-tonic-clonic) fall, and no tongue-biting or incontinence make an epileptic seizure very unlikely.
An emotional visual trigger rather than onset immediately on rising, and (sealed clinician finding) no significant active-stand BP drop, argue against orthostatic hypotension.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the red-flag screen this is uncomplicated benign vasovagal syncope: reassure, teach counter-pressure manoeuvres and hydration, recommend routine GP review with a baseline ECG for this first faint, and give explicit safety-netting for a future dangerous (exertional/unheralded/cardiac) faint and for any delayed head-injury symptoms. Continuing to lead with seizure or an emergency cardiac diagnosis after this benign constellation, or over-triaging a recovered patient to an ambulance, are both errors.

differential.diagnosis: Vasovagal (reflex) syncope, uncomplicated - presumptive, pending in-person neurological/cardiac examination, active-stand orthostatic vitals and a baseline 12-lead ECG
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis for the telehealth context. The emotionally triggered reflex-faint pattern is unambiguous on history; a baseline ECG completes low-yield exclusion of an occult arrhythmic cause for a first syncope, and there is no injury from the fall to manage.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final telehealth framing is uncomplicated benign vasovagal syncope - reassure, teach counter-pressure manoeuvres and hydration to prevent recurrence, recommend routine GP review with a baseline ECG, and safety-net for any future exertional/no-warning faint, palpitations or chest pain, and for delayed head-injury symptoms after the fall.

red_flags.description: Dangerous-syncope features (exertional or no-warning onset, palpitations, chest pain) - ABSENT but must be actively screened
red_flags.clinical_significance: The absence of exertional/unheralded onset, palpitations and chest pain supports a benign reflex mechanism; had any been present, an arrhythmic/structural cardiac cause would require urgent/emergency cardiac assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If a cardiac red flag were present and not screened, a life-threatening arrhythmia or structural cause of syncope could be missed.

red_flags.description: Family history of sudden or unexplained death at a young age - ABSENT but must be screened
red_flags.clinical_significance: A family history of premature sudden death would raise inheritable arrhythmia/cardiomyopathy syndromes (long QT, Brugada, HCM, CPVT/ARVC) and mandate cardiology evaluation; its absence is reassuring.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to screen could miss an inheritable sudden-death syndrome presenting as a first syncope.

red_flags.description: Fall during the syncope with a potential head strike - screened and reported negative (fell onto carpet, no injury felt), but delayed head-injury symptoms must still be safety-netted
red_flags.clinical_significance: Although no injury was found and the fall was onto carpet, any syncopal fall carries a small risk of an unrecognised head injury; delayed intracranial bleeding is the feared complication and warrants explicit safety-netting.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A subtle head injury or delayed intracranial bleed could be missed if the fall is not screened and head-injury safety-netting is not given.

red_flags.description: On clinician assessment: intact neurological examination, no significant active-stand orthostatic BP drop, dual heart sounds with no murmur, and no head trauma or laceration
red_flags.clinical_significance: These reassuring examination findings (normal cranial nerves/power/sensation/coordination, no nystagmus, no tongue-biting, negative orthostatic vitals, no murmur, no palpable head trauma) require an in-person clinician and cannot be obtained via telehealth chat; they support the benign diagnosis.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth - these findings require in-person examination; the correct action is reassurance with routine GP review and a baseline ECG.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Accepting the benign label and the patient's reassurance-seeking without actively screening dangerous-syncope features (exertional/no-warning onset, palpitations, chest pain, family history of sudden death) and seizure features - the screen is what makes the benign conclusion safe.

Sending a fully recovered young adult with a classic emotionally triggered reflex faint and no red flags to an emergency department by ambulance, rather than reassuring and arranging routine GP review with a baseline ECG.

Labelling the event as a seizure despite the classic autonomic prodrome, marked pallor, limp (non-tonic-clonic) fall, rapid recovery without postictal confusion, and absence of tongue-biting/incontinence.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Skipping the fall/injury check and head-injury safety-netting
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Focusing only on the cause of the faint and failing to confirm no injury from the fall or to give delayed head-injury safety-netting, even though no injury was detected here.
Ordering tilt-table testing or extensive cardiac work-up for a classic, uncomplicated first vasovagal episode, when a baseline ECG and reassurance are sufficient.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Neurological examination (cranial nerves, power, sensation, coordination, pupils, nystagmus, tongue for bite marks)
Assessment of the head and body for any occult trauma or laceration from the fall
Cardiac auscultation for murmurs and confirmation of regular rate and rhythm
Standardised active-stand orthostatic blood-pressure and heart-rate measurement
A baseline resting 12-lead ECG to exclude an occult arrhythmic substrate (e.g. long QT, pre-excitation, Brugada pattern)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Reassure that this is a classic benign reflex (vasovagal) faint and can be managed at home; teach physical counter-pressure manoeuvres (leg-crossing and muscle tensing at the first warning) and advise good hydration and avoiding the specific visual triggers; recommend routine GP review with a baseline 12-lead ECG as a low-yield check for a first syncope (tilt-table testing is not indicated for a classic uncomplicated first episode). Give explicit safety-netting - seek urgent/emergency care for a future faint that is exertional, without warning, or with chest pain/palpitations, and call an ambulance (000) for delayed head-injury symptoms (severe or worsening headache, repeated vomiting, drowsiness/confusion, weakness, seizure) after the fall.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The two errors are (1) reassuring without screening the dangerous-syncope features and without confirming no injury from the fall, and (2) over-triaging a fully recovered classic reflex faint with no red flags to an emergency ambulance; the benign conclusion is only safe once the cardiac/seizure screen is negative and injury has been excluded.
